title: An Eye for an Eye
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: An Eye for an Eye - Pastor Tito Lyro - Matthew 5_38-48.txt

outline.title: Introduction: The Sermon on the Mount and Human Nature
subpoints: Many people say they admire the Sermon on the Mount as words to live by — but the last verse demands: 'Be perfect, just as your Father in heaven is perfect'
We can only be perfect as God is perfect through faith in Christ, who perfectly kept the law and whose righteousness is imputed to believers
Jesus is not giving new laws here but correcting what religious leaders had twisted to make the law of God manageable and self-serving
The refrain 'you have heard it said' refers to what the religious teachers said, not the Old Testament itself — Jesus corrects their misreading
The word 'radical' means 'at the root' — Jesus is returning to the root of what it means to follow God: love

outline.title: Radical Love Responds to Wrongs with Ministry, Not Retaliation
subpoints: Verse 44: 'Love your enemies, bless those who curse you, do good to those who hate you, and pray for those who spitefully use you'
The common teaching was to love your neighbor and hate your enemy — a human distortion that allowed people to call anyone they disliked an 'enemy' and exempt them from love
Jesus assumes that his followers will have enemies: Matthew 5:10-12 promises persecution for the righteous; 2 Timothy 3:12 confirms it
Our enemies exist not because of our obnoxious behavior but because our righteous lives convict sinners who respond by trying to bring us down
Love is not primarily a feeling but an action: it thinks of the enemy as a fellow sinner who needs the forgiveness Christ provides

outline.title: Four Expressions of Radical Love Toward Enemies
subpoints: Love your enemies — think of them with genuine compassion as sinners headed for hell without Christ; Jesus looked at the crowds opposing him with compassion (Mark 6), not contempt
Bless those who curse you — if someone verbally attacks you, respond with kind words; a soft answer turns away wrath (Proverbs 15:1)
Do good to those who hate you — it is not enough to refrain from retaliation; we must actively do good; this is what God did for us while we were still sinners (Romans 5:6-10)
Pray for those who spitefully use you — sincerely pray for God's blessing and if applicable their conversion; Jesus prayed from the cross 'Father forgive them' and thousands were saved at Pentecost
The seven sayings from the cross: 'Father, forgive them, for they do not know what they are doing' — some of those people were saved weeks later

outline.title: Turning the Other Cheek: What It Means and What It Does Not
subpoints: Turning the other cheek does not make Christians into pacifists — John the Baptist told soldiers to fulfill their office; Romans 13 gives the state the sword for punishment
Jesus himself strongly confronted the Pharisees (Matthew 23), drove out the money changers, and rebuked his disciples — biblical love is not being a doormat
The right cheek: being struck on the right cheek requires a backhanded slap — this was an ancient gesture of insult rather than assault; Jesus is addressing personal affronts
The instruction is against impulsive retaliation driven by pride — the desire to make sure no one messes with you and gets away with it
An abused wife should call the police, then the elders — turning the other cheek does not mean silently enduring physical violence; a husband who abuses his wife demonstrates he does not know Christ

outline.title: Radical Love Treats Others as God Treats Us
subpoints: Verse 45: 'That you may be sons of your Father in heaven; for He makes His sun rise on the evil and on the good, and sends rain on the just and on the unjust'
God is merciful and benevolent to the just and the unjust alike; unbelievers eat from the produce of rain God sends while shaking their fists at him
Verses 46-47: even tax collectors love those who love them; radical love exceeds the world's standard by loving those who do not love back
We do not look to the world to learn how to love — we look to Christ
Verse 48: 'Be perfect, just as your Father in heaven is perfect' — perfection is only possible in Christ, whose righteousness covers us and whose Spirit enables us

outline.title: Application: Be Christ to Someone This Week
subpoints: Think of someone who has wronged you this week — pray for an opportunity to do something kind for that person
Let God's radical love that found you as a sinner flow through you to those who have mistreated you
We have experienced the mercy of God while we were enemies of God — it is our turn to extend that mercy to those who are opposed to us
The life we now live is not our own — we have been crucified with Christ; we are dead to self, so retaliation loses its power
Be Christ to them this week — and see what God will do through you by the power of his Spirit

summary:
Preaching from Matthew 5:38-48, Pastor Tito Lyro examines Jesus's teaching on radical love in the Sermon on the Mount — a passage often admired as fine ethical teaching until one reaches the closing demand to be as perfect as God the Father. The sermon begins by establishing that Jesus is not abolishing or replacing Old Testament law but correcting the religious leaders' distortions of it. The formula 'you have heard it said' refers to the traditions of the scribes and Pharisees, not to Moses. Jesus is going back to the root — the radical — of what following God means: a love that loves even enemies.

The sermon unpacks four specific expressions of love toward enemies commanded in verse 44: love your enemies with active compassion (thinking of them as sinners who need Christ, not merely obstacles to your comfort); bless those who curse you by responding to verbal attacks with kind words; do good to those who hate you by actively serving them; and pray for those who persecute you — sincerely, for their blessing and conversion. The model is Jesus himself, who prayed from the cross 'Father, forgive them,' and whose prayer was answered weeks later when thousands who had called for his crucifixion were converted at Pentecost.

Pastor Lyro addresses common misreadings of 'turn the other cheek,' making clear it does not require pacifism (John the Baptist affirmed soldiers' calling; Romans 13 gives the state the sword) or passive acceptance of ongoing abuse (an abused wife should call the police and then the elders). The backhanded slap to the right cheek was an ancient insult, and Jesus is primarily addressing how we respond to personal affronts driven by wounded pride. The sermon closes by grounding the call to radical love in the character of God himself — who sends rain on the just and unjust — and inviting the congregation to identify someone who has wronged them and do something kind for that person this week.
transcript:
## Scripture Reading: Matthew 5:38-48

If you are able, would you remain standing? We are turning to Matthew chapter 5, not Ephesians 5 as was in the bulletin — I worked really hard on Ephesians 5:6-14, but it would just not yield into the shape of a sermon. Lord willing, next Lord's Day we will consider that passage. Meanwhile today we will consider Matthew 5:38 through 48.

*You have heard that it was said, 'An eye for an eye and a tooth for a tooth.' But I tell you not to resist an evil person. But whoever slaps you on your right cheek, turn the other to him also. If anyone wants to sue you and take away your tunic, let him have your cloak also. And whoever compels you to go one mile, go with him two. Give to him who asks you, and from him who wants to borrow from you do not turn away. You have heard that it was said, 'You shall love your neighbor and hate your enemy.' But I say to you, love your enemies, bless those who curse you, do good to those who hate you, and pray for those who spitefully use you and persecute you, that you may be sons of your Father in heaven; for He makes His sun rise on the evil and on the good, and sends rain on the just and on the unjust. For if you love those who love you, what reward have you? Do not even the tax collectors do the same? And if you greet your brethren only, what do you do more than others? Do not even the tax collectors do so? Therefore you shall be perfect, just as your Father in heaven is perfect.*

This is the Word of our Lord. Let us pray. Father, we pray that you would speak to us from this passage. We pray that you would enlighten us by your Spirit, that we might see glorious things concerning you in it. We pray in Jesus' name. Amen. Please be seated.

## Introduction: The Most Demanding Verse in the Sermon

It is a difficult passage — not in its complexity but in its requirement, especially if you begin with the last verse: be perfect as God is perfect. Many people say they do not care much for the Bible or for 'the Jesus thing,' but they really care for the Sermon on the Mount: these are words to live by, they say. But how do you be perfect as God is perfect?

You can only do that with the totality of the Scriptures, because you read elsewhere that those who come to faith in Jesus are declared to be perfect — not on their own doing, but on the merits of Christ, who is the perfect one who perfectly kept the law of God, who died the death we deserved, and who rose again to life so that we might have life. The life we now live is not our own — Christ lives in us, because we have been crucified with him.

So how are you perfect as God is perfect? Not by your doing, but by your believing.

It is a discouragement to see how human nature grabs hold of what God says. James calls the Bible the Royal Law of Liberty — God gave us the law for our freedom. It seems counterintuitive that the law of God would be given for our liberty, and yet human beings grab these things God gave us for good and twist them for their own purposes.

The Sermon on the Mount — particularly chapter 5 — is Jesus's correction of what the religious leaders had twisted concerning the Word of God. Jesus is not changing things. He is not necessarily giving us new laws. He is telling us that the religious leaders of the day had twisted the things God had given them to their own benefit.

Notice the refrain: 'You have heard it said.' In the Sermon on the Mount, when Jesus says 'you have heard it said,' he is not quoting the Old Testament. That is not the formula he uses to quote the Old Testament in Matthew — for that he uses 'it is written' or 'this was done that it might be fulfilled.' When he says 'you have heard it said,' he is referring to what the scribes and Pharisees said. And he says: they were wrong. They twisted the Scriptures.

Humankind does that because we cannot do everything God calls us to do. We want to be the captains of our ships, in charge of our own destiny. We are Americans after all — we pull ourselves up by our bootstraps. We are not willing to have our eternal destiny in the hands of someone else. So humankind comes up with its own rules that are keepable, like the Pharisees did, so we can say: 'See, God, we obeyed. Look at the list. Now you must accept me.'

The problem is, if that list is the Word of God, you are not going to be able to claim it before God. The best way to know that you cannot obey God perfectly is to try. Try for a week. Try for two hours to really focus on the Word of God and do everything it says. You will see that apart from the grace of Jesus Christ, it is not possible. The only way to obey God is through faith in Jesus Christ, because he has obeyed God for us.

So Jesus is here being radical in the original sense of that word. The word *radical* means 'at the root.' What Jesus is doing here is returning to the root of what it means to follow God: love. A follower of Jesus Christ loves the people in his or her life — even those who are his or her enemies. That is what Jesus is calling us to in this passage: radical love that loves even those who have afflicted us.

It is a difficult passage, but not a complicated one. And yet if you are honest, you see that apart from the grace of Jesus Christ, there is no way you can live by it.

Before we go verse by verse, I want to say a couple things about the passage as a whole to prevent misunderstanding. Several people have misunderstood this passage to mean that God calls us to be pacifists — that we cannot have a military, cannot have police. That is not the right interpretation. Remember that when soldiers came to John the Baptist asking what they should do to show their repentance, he did not say 'stop being a soldier.' He said: fulfill your office as a soldier as God calls you. We cannot read this and conclude God calls everyone to be a pacifist — that would ignore the entire Old Testament, where God directed Israel to fight many wars. Romans 13 gives the state the sword to inflict punishment.

So we allow for hyperbole. Jesus is using a valid figure of speech — bringing things to an extreme to make a point. But we must not throw everything away either. Jesus wants us to have the attitude that if we need to, we will give the clothes off our back, turn the other cheek, give everything to those in need even if they are our enemies. That radical attitude must be our attitude — because we have been crucified with Christ, and the life we now live is not our own. We are dead to self. If we are dead, it doesn't matter how many times people hit us.

I think Jesus is teaching four categories in this passage. We may only get through two today, and we will return to the others.

## 1. Radical Love Responds to Wrongs with Ministry Toward the Wrongdoer, Not Retaliation

Look at verse 44: *But I say to you, love your enemies, bless those who curse you, do good to those who hate you, and pray for those who spitefully use you and persecute you.*

(Note: if you are using the ESV, NIV, or NASB, some of what I just read may not appear in that verse as those translations follow slightly different manuscript traditions — but the full content is also in Luke chapter 6, so it is the Word of God and we will consider it.)

The common teaching of the religious leaders was: love your neighbor, hate your enemy. A human distortion designed to make the law keepable. What do we do? Enemy, enemy, enemy — anyone we do not want to love becomes our enemy, and we can stand before God and say: I obeyed. But Jesus says: No. That is not what the Bible says.

Now, though Christians can sometimes consider other Christians enemies, the Bible never classifies a brother or sister as our enemy. When Jesus says 'love your enemies,' he is primarily speaking of unbelievers who are against us. And notice that Jesus assumes you and I are going to have enemies. He says this in the Beatitudes: *Blessed are those who are persecuted for righteousness' sake, for theirs is the kingdom of heaven* (Matthew 5:10-12). A promise to believers: you will be persecuted. You will have enemies. The world will be in enmity with Christians.

We should not have enemies because of our obnoxious or insensitive behavior. There is no virtue in just being a jerk. The enemies Jesus is talking about are there because you are faithful to the Lord. If we live righteously and hold firmly to God's truth, we will have enemies in this evil world. Why? Because our lives convict sinners, who will try to bring us down to justify their own sin. We live in an age called 'the age of tolerance,' but only one position is actually permitted, and you will be socially destroyed for disagreeing. The world hates Christianity because the world hates Christ. Our Lord said: the world will hate you because it hated me first.

How do we respond? We respond to all mistreatment by actively loving those who have wronged us — and never by retaliating. That is what this passage says. It is not complicated to understand, but it is so hard to do.

**Love your enemies.** This love is not primarily a feeling but an action that stems from an attitude. The attitude of love thinks about the other person as a fellow sinner who needs the forgiveness that Jesus provides. How often do we look at our enemies that way? How often do we look at them with genuine compassion — with the thought: you need Christ, let me give you Christ, let me tell you about Christ?

That is what Jesus did. Mark tells us that when he looked at the crowds — crowds including people who would eventually shout 'Crucify him!' — he looked at them with great compassion. His stomach was in a knot when he saw the unbelieving crowd wandering like sheep without a shepherd. And he said: the fields are white for harvest — pray the Lord of the harvest to send workers.

Remember one of the seven sayings from the cross: as the Roman soldiers are there, as the Jewish leaders are there, as the people who had shouted 'crucify him' the day before are there — Jesus prays: *Father, forgive them, for they do not know what they are doing.* Did the Father honor that prayer? Yes. Just a few weeks later at Pentecost, thousands of those same people were saved and forgiven for crucifying Jesus.

We have experienced the mercy of God. It is now our turn to extend that mercy — that love — to those who are opposed to us.

**Do good to those who hate you.** It is not enough to refrain from retaliation. It is not enough just to bite your tongue. It is not enough not to punch them in the face. It is not sufficient to separate yourself from the one who has wronged you. Jesus says: you must actively do good to the wrongdoer. And you may say: how can Jesus require that? What do you think he did for you? Do you think Jesus looked down from heaven and said, 'I need that person, I can't live without them, that's why I'll save them'? No. God's love was demonstrated in that *while we were still sinners, Christ died for us* (Romans 5:8). Christ willingly died for us when we were shaking our fists at God. If you are a believer, you have experienced that love. That is all Christ is saying: love others with the same love with which I have loved you.

**Bless those who curse you.** If a person verbally attacks you, respond with kind words. Sometimes a curse can be very quick in forming in the mind — some of you have experienced that from me, and for that I am very sorry. Jesus says: do not pay back the same way. If somebody attacks you with words, bless them. The Bible says: *A soft answer turns away wrath* (Proverbs 15:1). My wife is a master of the soft answer. I am the master of wrath. And she often disarms me by her gracious response.

**Pray for those who spitefully use you.** Not imprecatory prayers — not 'may your children dash against the rocks.' Pray for blessings of God upon them. Pray sincerely for their well-being, which may include their conversion. We can rest assured that if the person does not repent, God will bring his righteous judgment in due time. We do not have to be the judge. We do not have to execute the verdict. God will vindicate his people.

So rather than feeling sorry for ourselves because we have been mistreated, we feel compassion for this sinner who is headed for hell if God does not intervene. Remember that the sinner is acting toward you the only way he or she can — as an unbeliever. They cannot act any other way. But you and I have been empowered by the Spirit, and sin no longer has dominion over us. Pray that God would be merciful in saving the person for his glory.

## 2. Turning the Other Cheek

This is often wrongly interpreted to mean a Christian should never defend himself against aggression. But Jesus was not talking about governmental force or military — as we have seen. Even Romans 13 tells us the state holds the sword for punishment.

Neither does Jesus mean we should never confront those who are in sin — he drove the money changers out of the temple, strongly confronted the Pharisees in Matthew 23, rebuked his disciples, and said to Peter: 'Get behind me, Satan.' Biblical love does not mean being a doormat.

Turning the other cheek does not mean a wife should silently endure physical abuse from an evil husband. She can and must confront his sin in the proper spirit. If it continues, call the authorities that God has ordained for her protection. Husbands — if you think your office gives you permission to be abusive to your family, you do not know Christ. That is not what Christ does to his church. His church is never beaten or abused. Women in the church: if you are being abused by your husband, your first call is to the police. Have him arrested. Your second call is to the elders. In that order — so that righteousness may rule in your home.

Now, do you notice how Jesus says 'if someone strikes you on your right cheek'? Most people are right-handed. In order to strike someone on the *right* cheek, if you are right-handed, you have to deliver a backhanded blow. For centuries, a backhand was considered a gesture of insult rather than physical assault. So Jesus is talking more about being insulted — about someone demeaning you — than about a physical beating.

And so his instruction is: do not respond to insults with pride-driven retaliation — with the need to prove that no one messes with you and gets away with it. That stems from self-interested pride. Do not have a temper that is quick to prove nobody can touch you.

When Jesus commands us to offer the other cheek, he is not saying literally to allow yourself to be struck twice. He wants us not to be reactive and impulsive. And the same applies to the cloak: we have no record of Jesus walking naked. He is not saying literally to give someone everything including your underwear. But be ready to provide for people who need your help — even your enemies.

## 3. Radical Love Treats Others as God Treats Us

Look at verse 45: *That you may be sons of your Father in heaven; for He makes His sun rise on the evil and on the good, and sends rain on the just and on the unjust.*

God is merciful to the just and the unjust. He sends rain and sunshine to those who shake their fists at him, who eat the food his rain produces while denying him. And we are the sons and daughters of that God — and we are to demonstrate that family resemblance by acting as he acts.

Quickly, a third category: **radical love that exceeds the world's standard.** Verses 46-47: if you love those who love you, even tax collectors do that. Tax collectors were the ultimate sinners in Jesus's time. If your love extends no further than those who already love you back, you are at the same level as the worst people in the society. The Spirit indwells you — you can do better than that. We do not look to the world to learn how to love. We look to Christ.

And lastly: **radical love shows the nature of God to people who desperately need him.** We display our status as sons and daughters of God when we love those who despise us. And that opens the door to proclaim the gospel. Why do we do these things? Because that is exactly what God did for us. While we were still sinners, Christ died for us.

## Application

Let me challenge you as we finish. This week: think of someone who has wronged you. Pray for an opportunity to do something kind for him or for her. Pray for an opportunity to demonstrate love to them. Let God's radical love — the love that found you while you were a sinner — flow through you to those who have mistreated you because of his love for you. Find a concrete way to demonstrate that. Be Christ to them this week and see what God will do through you as he works in you through the power of his Spirit.

Thank you, Father, that you speak to us in your Word. We pray that you give us the grace to be obedient to you. We thank you that you have given us the Spirit to empower us to obey you. Help us to rely on him, help us to die to ourselves as we follow you. We pray in Jesus' name. Amen.
